IC.rationale: Core account remains stable across the two-episode testimony. The witness maintains consistent description of the 45-minute window encounter, the creature's appearance, behavior, and his emotional state. However, some minor temporal inconsistencies emerge: he states the encounter lasted '45 minutes less than a half away' (unclear phrasing), then later references watching 'for those 45 50 minutes until that rest of the two hours.' The phrase 'rest of the two hours' is vague about whether he watched the creature for 2 hours total or something else. Additionally, there are some contradictions about what he could see of the creature's body: he initially says he 'didn't see the body' but then discusses details about its 'peanut butter cardboard color' fur and hand/arm proportions. These are minor memory artifacts rather than fundamental contradictions.
evidence_quotes: 45 minutes less than i put a half away from that thing
for those 45 50 minutes until that rest of the two hours i watched
i didn't see the body that's one of the strange many strange things
fur was beautiful it was nice peanut butter cardboard color

SS.rationale: Rich sensory detail across multiple channels. Strong visual specificity regarding eye color (dark brown with white in corners), nose (solid black), fur color and texture (peanut butter/cardboard color, groomed appearance, not shaggy or muddy), ear positioning and length (7-8 inches, more on top of head), hand/finger morphology (long, bent inward, three joints, resembling stretched dog paws). Kinesthetic detail: describes paralysis sensation, eye-locking intensity, the feel of being watched. Touch implied through hand-scratching-screen description. Minimal olfactory or auditory detail (no sounds reported, no smell mentioned). Strong peripheral context: sleeping arrangement with brothers, window size/position, screen condition, tapping that woke him. Comparison to his dog Zero provides grounding detail.
evidence_quotes: blackish brown eyes and when i say it looked exactly like zero eyes and just i could see just a little bit of white in them on both corners of his eye
fur was beautiful it was nice peanut butter cardboard color it was not it just seemed like it was groomed
i would put this ear at about seven inches seven to eight inches... more on top of his head
they were long and bent inwards... bent forward... just really long folded over like that
i was laying back almost on top of one of my brothers... looking at that one until i fell asleep

SA.rationale: Witness volunteers multiple unprompted details and makes self-corrections. He spontaneously clarifies his own timeline confusion ('45 minutes less than a half away'), corrects his description of what he could see by comparing to Zero's posture unprompted, and acknowledges uncertainty throughout ('I can't think for this thing. All I can do is just guess'). He admits confusion about hand anatomy ('almost like it's formed a hand like it's just a dog paw'). However, much of the dialogue is reactive to host questions rather than purely spontaneous narrative. The account is largely linear despite some non-sequential jumping between topics. Some statements feel slightly rehearsed given this is his second episode appearance ('I know for a fact at least it was one...that it's worth at least one of those things').
evidence_quotes: I can't think for this thing. All I can do is just guess.
that's what tricked me... didn't at first didn't recognize this now until like i say when zero did
i couldn't i could not match it up with anything because like i said it had his head down
Nothing strange about blackish brown eyes
maybe it pinned it sears just because of the intensity of the moment

EA.rationale: Emotional content is present but somewhat muted and not strongly anchored to specific narrative moments. The witness reports initial shock ('unbelief... shifted from unbelief to what is this') and notes he 'wasn't scared' multiple times. He shows emotional investment in the outcome ('I'm so glad it did because it put my mind at rest') and describes a 40-year period of wondering. However, there are no emotional breaks, voice quality shifts, or physiological stress markers (tremor, crying, voice cracking) evident in the transcript. He describes paralysis-like state ('it was like i was paralyzed like it was paralyzed') but without the emotional escalation typically seen in genuine trauma recall. The disclosure delay is significant (40+ years before the dog Zero helped him process it), which supports authenticity, but the current retelling feels more reflective than emotionally activated. The closing statement shows conviction but lacks the affective intensity that characterizes genuine trauma re-engagement.
evidence_quotes: the very first feeling that came to me when I when I first set up it was unbelief
it shifted from unbelief to what is this
i wasn't scared because it is no teeth showing nothing no growling
it put my mind at ease... i was so glad
that was 40 a little bit over 40 years and and all that time i wonder what was it

CP.rationale: Context is highly plausible. Legitimate reason for presence: staying at grandfather's house as a child, sleeping with brothers in same room. Geography internally consistent: rural Georgia setting with pond, ditch, woods, multiple family properties within walking/visiting distance (mentions grandparents, Mr. L 100 yards away, another location 600 yards down, separate grandparents' house). Activity/time plausible: sleeping at night in a bedroom with window access. Season coherent: references ongoing visits to the location, hunting/fishing in the area. The old house with minimal security (nail-through-wood door latch) is consistent with rural 1980s dwelling. No timeline contradictions regarding the setting itself. The only minor issue is vagueness about exact year/date, but this is normal for childhood memories from 40+ years prior.
evidence_quotes: I'm a simple country boy... moved to texas a big city
staying at my grandfather's house... me and my brother stayed down there
old house back there... they took a nail and took about a six foot i mean a six inch by two inch little block of wood
like i say when we visited back up there... i always slept over there by that window
hunting fishing climbing trees... down there in that ditch... by that pond

CO.rationale: Limited but meaningful corroboration. The witness mentions telling his brother (who was present in the room) about the encounter, though the brother's account is not independently verified in this transcript. He references his grandmother responding with belief ('that's what you saw'), suggesting some immediate family corroboration. He mentions learning of a Mr. Rachel's Sasquatch encounter at a supermarket, and a separate unnamed person's encounter his grandmother told him about—both providing social corroboration that other encounters exist in the region, but not corroboration of his specific event. The most credible corroboration is the brother's presence during the event (mentioned in the account: 'my brothers laying beside me,' 'my sister my youngest brother'). However, no independent brother testimony is presented. The dog Zero serves as a physical comparison tool but not as corroboration of the encounter itself. No physical evidence presented (no photos of damaged screen, damaged window frame, or other forensic proof).
evidence_quotes: my brothers laying beside me
i spoke to my grandmother about it and she brought it up
she told me about an encounter she had with something
Mr. Rachel... was telling about you know he had an encounter with a Sasquatch
my older brother... I guarantee if we really wanted to it may have took a little while we probably could have went through that wall

notable_patterns: Eye contact held for entire 45-50 minute duration without breaks—highly unusual claim, difficult to fake but also neurologically improbable for human sustained focus
Creature's unresponsiveness to potential triggers (no teeth display, no vocalization, no aggressive movement) positioned as 'lucky lottery'—suggests victim mentality typical of genuine witnesses
Detailed hand morphology description with three-joint structure—consistent with multiple dogman encounter reports in literature, suggests either genuine observation or sophisticated confabulation
Creature never moved except for initial ear pinning and following witness's eye movement—stasis narrative common in high-strangeness accounts
40+ year disclosure delay before processing trauma through dog ownership—authentic trauma response pattern
Repeated self-comparison to dog Zero as memory aid—authentic memory reconstruction technique
Host-directed interview format may suppress spontaneous narrative flow and emotional authenticity
minimisation_phrases: i'm pretty much glad i did
at least it was one of those things
accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the MODERATE range (6.6/10 WCS). Strengths: exceptional sensory specificity across visual, kinesthetic, and comparative domains; significant disclosure delay (40 years); post-encounter life impact (dog ownership as processing mechanism); some unprompted self-correction; plausible context with peripheral detail; reported presence of corroborating brother. Weaknesses: emotional authenticity muted and not strongly narrative-anchored; limited independent corroboration; second-interview format may suppress spontaneity; some temporal vagueness ('45 minutes less than a half away'); unclear phrasing about body visibility. The account reads as generally consistent with genuine experiential memory but lacks the affective intensity and micro-temporal precision that would elevate it to 'Strong' range. The 40-year delay and dog-triggered resolution is noteworthy as authentic trauma processing pattern. Host-directed interview structure limits assessment of pure spontaneity.

IC.rationale: Core account is structurally stable across two main dogman encounters and one gugwe encounter. However, there are notable inconsistencies in the taxonomy/classification system Thomas uses. He describes 'werewolves' as distinct from gugwe and canine dogmen, but then describes them with overlapping anatomical features (bipedal, humanlike gait, wolf head). The thermal imaging encounter where 'the Bigfoot started screaming' and the werewolf 'took off' introduces a causal claim but lacks clarity on spatial relationships. Minor timeline ambiguity: he says the thermal encounter was 'last October' but the podcast itself is undated, making verification impossible. The Land Between the Lakes account is internally consistent (narrow trail, vertical drops, progressive surveillance, tree knocks, departure), but the State Park account is vague on the creature's actual behavior departure.
evidence_quotes: My other one was in Land Between the Lakes last April...we were being trailed...a few minutes later there was some brush...it came up the rock face behind this bush...I yelled at it...It got the message and it scurried on down to the creek
When we're watching this thing walk, the Bigfoot did frequency his property, they started screaming. And when the Bigfoot started screaming, they took off. the dog, the werewolf took off.
werewolf...walks like us. It's bipedal like us. it doesn't have the hawks it has the same lower body structure as we do but it's got a giant wolf's head on it
the gugway's complete is completely different because that gugway they have the small leaders like a big foot uh their snout is more uh more like a baboon

SS.rationale: Thomas provides moderate sensory specificity, primarily visual with some kinesthetic and auditory elements. The State Park encounter is sparse: 'amber eyes staring at me...going up up up at least eight feet...it looked at me for a minute...it dropped back down to all fours and it left in a loud manner you could tell it was heavy.' The Land Between the Lakes account has better spatial/environmental detail (4-foot trail, 300-foot drops, creek, brush, rock face) and auditory elements (tree knocks every 60 seconds, sounds of movement). The werewolf thermal account relies entirely on visual-thermal interpretation with no sensory texture ('just massive'). Missing: smell, taste, tactile proximity sensation, precise distance measurements, light conditions, ambient noise besides tree knocks. No description of his own physiological state (breathing, heartrate, muscle tension) during encounters.
evidence_quotes: I see this set of amber eyes staring at me...going up up up at least eight feet...it looked at me for a minute i looked at it for a minute it dropped back down to all fours and it left in a loud manner you could tell it was heavy

Devil's Backbone on the Tennessee side...your trail is only like maybe four feet across. And it's like 300 foot vertical drops on each side...we saw branches snapped off that weren't snapped off on the way in...ears, the big pointy ears. And it was just moving back and forth...we were getting tree knocks like every 60 seconds on either side
in the thermal, you could just tell it was just massive

SA.rationale: Thomas's account is largely reactive to host questions and lacks hallmark spontaneous corrections or uncertainty admissions. He answers direct prompts with relatively polished, linear narratives without volunteering tangential details or second-guessing himself. No instances of 'I could be wrong' or 'actually, let me correct that' or 'I'm not entirely sure but...' He does acknowledge one limit: 'I have no idea where the Wojo mines are even at' and later 'I have no idea where that's at'—but these are deflections rather than spontaneous detail refinement. The account follows a clear Q&A structure throughout. He delivers advice and taxonomy as established doctrine rather than reconstructing memory in real time. No self-corrections. No 'I remember now' moments. One slight unscripted moment: 'You're reminding me of the werewolf from Van Helsing / Uh oh / That's not good / Someone's not happy'—but this is conversational reaction, not narrative spontaneity.
evidence_quotes: I have no idea where the Wojo mines are even at. I don't either.
Well, my research partner at the time looked at me goes what was that i looked at him and said you don't want to know
[Host asks direct question, Thomas provides structured answer throughout]
No spontaneous unprompted details offered; all responses follow host prompts

EA.rationale: Emotional response is minimal and lacks narrative anchoring. There is an extended interruption where 'the wife' is heard in background and Thomas says 'She doesn't sound very happy. I apologize for the pause'—but this appears to be domestic rather than trauma-related. Thomas reports witnessing a creature that 'completely change[s] your life. Everything you've ever thought, never believed in will come crashing down'—yet he himself describes matter-of-fact interactions ('I yelled at it...It got the message'). His own encounters are narrated with flat affect: no voice breaks, no pauses for composure, no tremor in recounting threat moments. He says 'I don't fear them. I respect them'—a cognitive framing that may suppress emotional release. The hellhound sighting ('it just dissipated in front of me. Literally. In the smoke') is treated as a categorical statement, not a moment of recalled shock. Zero physiological stress descriptors (trembling, nausea, time distortion, paralysis). The closest to emotional intensity is the anecdote about 'a person here that doesn't live far from me that got terrorized...for about two hours...hasn't been back in the woods since'—but this is about *someone else*, not Thomas himself.
evidence_quotes: I don't fear them. I respect them. I respect what they're capable of doing.
it completely change your life. Everything you've ever thought, never believed in will come crashing down.
And it just dissipated in front of me. Literally. In the smoke. [No emotional marker]
Well, my research partner at the time looked at me goes what was that i looked at him and said you don't want to know [delivered without noted vocal inflection or pause]
[Wife interrupts] She doesn't sound very happy. I apologize for the pause [domestic, not trauma-related]

CP.rationale: Context is plausible overall. Thomas is a former military policeman with 30 years investigating cryptids—a coherent biographical frame. State Park in southern Indiana is a real geography where he was 'setting up camera looking for Bigfoot' with 'a couple other people'—legitimate reason to be in woods at night. Land Between the Lakes is a real protected area on Tennessee-Kentucky border; Devil's Backbone is an identifiable hiking trail with known topography (4-foot width, 300-foot drops match real geography). April timeframe is plausible. The thermal imaging at 'his house' (or research partner's) in October is contextually routine. Minor plausibility issue: he describes the pack theory ('There's been packs of up to 30 of these things running together') but offers no source or detail. The gugwe encounter is mentioned but *never actually narrated*—only its appearance is described in response to questions. This is a significant gap: he was asked 'tell us about your Gugwe encounter' and deflected into taxonomy instead. The host notices this ('I'm going to ask you in just a bit here to tell us about your Gugwe encounter') but never follows up to elicit the actual narrative.
evidence_quotes: I'm a former military policeman in the United States Army for 13 years. Done two tours in the sandbox.
I was out there with a couple other people setting up camera looking for Bigfoot
And I was with two other people. I was down in the Devil's Backbone on the Tennessee side.
[Gugwe encounter never actually narrated; only physical description given]
There's been packs of up to 30 of these things running together [unsourced claim]

CO.rationale: Limited corroboration. State Park encounter: 'my research partner at the time looked at me goes what was that'—one corroborating witness present but no independent verification offered. Land Between the Lakes: 'I was with two other people'—multiple witnesses present, but no names given and no follow-up confirmation indicated. The thermal imaging encounter allegedly involved 'my research partner, Neil'—named witness, potentially verifiable, but the transcript provides no statement from Neil or external confirmation. The host does not ask for witness names or contact information. No physical evidence presented (photos, thermal footage, tracks cast). No prior area reports cited. The gugwe encounter is so vaguely situated it offers no corroboration opportunity. Thomas references 'a person here that doesn't live far from me that got terrorized'—anecdotal third-party report, not direct corroboration of his own account. He cites Nick Valente and Hawk Spearman as 'go-to guys' in the field, but they are not quoted as corroborating his specific encounters.
evidence_quotes: my research partner at the time looked at me goes what was that
I was with two other people
my research partner, Neil, who is on the show, we were researching at his house last October. And we caught a werewolf on thermal.
[No names or contact info for State Park or Land Between the Lakes witnesses provided]
[Gugwe encounter location, date, witnesses entirely absent]

notable_patterns: No actual gugwe encounter narrative despite prompt—only taxonomy
Lack of emotional affect during reported life-changing events
Heavy reliance on expert credentials (military, 30 years) rather than memory detail
Taxonomy system unclear and internally inconsistent (werewolf/gugwe/canine boundaries blurred)
Multiple witnesses claimed but no independent testimony or verification
Advice and theory-building dominate over experiential reconstruction

it left in a loud manner

accounts.scoring_notes: This transcript is a podcast guest interview, not a direct witness testimony. Thomas functions as an expert commentator and field investigator offering theory, taxonomy, and advice rather than reconstructing traumatic personal memory in real time. The structural markers of genuine experiential memory (spontaneous detail, emotional breaks at narrative triggers, self-correction, sensory richness, physiological stress markers) are largely absent. His accounts are stable but spare, reactive rather than spontaneous, and delivered with flat affect. The failure to narrate the gugwe encounter despite explicit prompt is notable—suggests either it is less vivid in memory, or the interview format does not elicit genuine reconstruction. His framing as a credentialed expert (military, 30-year investigator) may substitute for phenomenological authenticity. The account scores low-to-moderate on markers of genuine traumatic memory but cannot be definitively scored as fabricated—it is simply not structured as experiential testimony. It reads as an expert briefing rather than a disclosure.

IC.rationale: Account is largely internally consistent across the three main dogman sightings (2007-2008 period). Core details remain stable: creature height (4.5 feet), appearance (dark gray, short hair, Great Dane/Greyhound build), behavior (clicking nails, calm demeanor, same bridge location, consistent routes). Minor inconsistencies exist: stride length estimates vary ('20 inches' then 'probably 20' with hedging), and some timeline vagueness ('about six months apart', 'about a year or so'). The witness acknowledges uncertainty naturally ('I didn't really have a yardstick'). One potential contradiction: he states the creature was 'almost eye level' while sitting on a bridge, then estimates 4.5 feet—plausible if bridge gave him elevation. The narrative structure shows some natural memory reconstruction rather than scripted perfection.
evidence_quotes: it was almost eye level to me, about four and a half foot tall
i didn't really have a yardstick at the time so i didn't really sit down and figure it out
Those two incidences happened about six months to each other
probably about, I don't know, a year or so

SS.rationale: Rich sensory specificity across multiple channels. Visual detail is strong and precise: creature height, build comparison (Great Dane/Greyhound), dark gray coloration under moonlight, short hair texture ('very short hair i mean it did not shaggy not no mane'). Auditory detail is compelling: 'faint tick, tick, tick, tick, tick of nails coming up the street'—specific, distinctive. Kinesthetic/proprioceptive: 'it was almost eye level to me', 'five foot away from me'. Environmental context rich: bridge location, creek, tree line, moonlight conditions, abandoned airstrip light source. Eyes encounter includes specific color ('aqua blue'), size ('eight inches apart'), and behavior ('blinking'). Peripheral detail adequate: his role as caregiver providing isolation context, the CSU fence as backdrop. Limited smell/touch channels, but compensated by strong detail in visual and auditory channels. Two sensory channels strongly developed, one moderately so.

I hear the faint tick, tick, tick, tick, tick of nails coming up the street from the west
very short hair i mean it did not shaggy not no mane no nothing very short hair all over uh i could tell it was like a dark gray i could tell that much because of the moonlight
two sets of eyes looking back at me... the entirety of the eye the whites you know everything was reflecting a um a light aqua blue um color

SA.rationale: Substantial spontaneity and authenticity markers. Witness volunteers rich unprompted details before being asked direct questions (creek, bridge, tree line, nails clicking sound, comparison to Great Dane). Self-corrections present: 'stride would have been...20, like what, probably about 20 inches...No, probably 20' shows genuine uncertainty reconstruction. Admits knowledge gaps explicitly: 'I'd really have to, you know, say no, I don't know' on stride measurements; 'I will have to, you know, claim ignorance on that' regarding tribal land. Non-linear memory structure—tangentially brings up bigfoot sighting at 16, cemetery encounter, police officer reaction. Shows genuine questioning of own experience: 'I'm still questioning it. It's just, I don't know' and 'But it makes no sense because it's nothing like I've been listening to.' Minimal resistance to uncertainty. Some reactive elements due to interview format, but substantial unprompted elaboration throughout.

i didn't really have a yardstick at the time. So i didn't really sit down and figure it out
is this fence here because of them? I mean, what's going on? What are they doing, CSU? What are you doing
the reaction that I was watching the faces on these two police officers, it was priceless
oh well, perhaps this is what I have been seeing, you know, coming down the street

EA.rationale: Emotional content present but inconsistently anchored to narrative moments and somewhat muted overall. Witness describes encounters as 'shocking' and 'flabbergasted' in real-time response, appropriate to context. However, emotional affect in the telling is relatively flat; he uses hedging language ('you know') extensively rather than conveying visceral emotion. No evidence of emotional breaks (crying, voice tremor). One strong emotional moment: laughter at police officers' reaction—but internally, not externally expressed. States 'I wasn't threatened by it or anything like that' and later 'non-threatening behavior'—seems to minimize emotional impact post-hoc. The one-time he ran ('chuck tail and run') shows physiological response, but limited detail. Post-impact life changes not clearly articulated—he returned to same location repeatedly. Disclosure was delayed significantly (2007-2008 sightings, started listening to podcast 2 years ago, only then reconsidered meaning). This delay is appropriate for genuine memory, but emotional processing of that delay is not expressed. Overall: emotional content fits narrative but lacks the physiological texture and authentic affect breaks of higher-scoring accounts.

I was like, oh, no, I don't want to see that thing. You know, I don't know what it is.
the reaction that I was watching the faces on these two police officers, it was priceless. I mean, I just started laughing. I mean, internally, you know?
being back to the house you know yeah chuck tail and run because that was not that was not anything that i would want to um run into
It was non-threatening. I mean, it was comical because, I mean, it was so laid back.

CP.rationale: Context is highly plausible and internally consistent. Witness had legitimate reason to be in location: employed as 24/7 caregiver for disabled adult; night was only personal time ('the only time that I really had to myself was at night'). Geography coherent: Northern Colorado, Fort Collins area, CSU Forestry research center, abandoned airstrip, Goat Hill, old cemetery—all named, creating verifiable specificity. Activity timing coherent: visiting same bridge/road location repeatedly for respite (natural behavior for isolated worker). Seasonal detail present ('especially during the summer'). Setting details plausible: creek, tree line, moonlight visibility, road corner turning points. Even implausible-sounding element (CSU fortress fence) is given logical question: 'is this fence here because of them?' showing witness reasoning. Police officer encounter in same area lends environmental realism. No major contradictions in when/where/why he was there. One minor contextual oddity: why would CSU have such a high-security fence for a forestry research center? But witness articulates his own puzzlement, which adds authenticity rather than detracting.
evidence_quotes: I was working for an agency that we took care of developmentally disabled adults... the only time that I really had to myself was at night
it's going to be 2007 to 2008 when I ran into this thing... there was a creek, there was a tree line, a creek, and then this little bridge
CSU Forestry has a research center down there and they have this fence around the property
The only light in the area was from an abandoned airstrip that was behind me
it's northern Colorado... 12 miles, I think, from where I literally grew up

CO.rationale: Partial corroboration present. Witness explicitly states friend was present during New Orleans paranormal encounter (not dogman-related, but shows he has corroborating witnesses for some experiences): 'I had a friend there, you know, with me too' and friend independently reported seeing entity. For the dogman encounters specifically: witness mentions police officers present during final sighting—'there was a police officer sitting, probably a cop car with two police officers... about 70, 80 feet away from me.' This is significant corroboration IF true, though not independently verified in transcript. Witness also states 'I had several friends that would be with me you know for a lot of this stuff' regarding paranormal phenomena generally, suggesting some witnesses exist. However, no police report cited, no names of officers given, no independent verification. The Goat Hill Ghost Lights phenomenon is referenced as having prior area reports ('apparently they've been recorded since the late 1890s') and published accounts ('gazette or something'), lending environmental credibility to the area's paranormal reputation. This provides modest environmental corroboration. No direct physical evidence (footprints, hair, etc.) reported. Solo encounters constitute the majority of dogman sightings.
evidence_quotes: there was a police officer sitting, probably a cop car with two police officers. and they were sitting about 70, 80 feet away from me
I had a friend there, you know, with me too... He was like, I'm a centric tall, a brown you know brown cloak and hoodie and he's like i saw its face
I had several friends that would be with me you know for a lot of this stuff
this phenomenon they call the Goat Hill Ghost Lights. And apparently they've been recorded since the late 1890s
No. Nope. No. Nothing. Nothing. Nope. No footprints. Nothing like that.

notable_patterns: Creature repeatedly returns to same location (bridge/road), suggesting habitual territory—consistent with genuine animal observation
Witness spontaneously questions official presence (CSU fence, base speculations) without explicit prompting—suggests genuine curiosity rather than narrative construction
Describes creature as 'comical', 'laid back', non-threatening, calm—departures from typical monster narrative tropes suggest authentic observation rather than genre-influenced storytelling
Extended isolation (6 years as sole caregiver) provides psychological context for heightened environmental attention—supports why observations would accumulate
Witness acknowledges learning about 'dogman' phenomenon only AFTER encounters, reduces risk of retroactive narrative contamination: 'I didn't even know about dog man until like 10 years later'
Specific sensory anomaly (aqua blue eye reflection) is unusual and unexpected—not a typical cryptid trope, suggests authentic perceptual memory
Admits to running away from two-legged creature encounter but not four-legged encounters—shows contextual fear response variation consistent with genuine experience
Repeating self-corrections on measurements ('20 inches', 'probably 20') suggests authentic memory uncertainty rather than memorized narrative
accounts.scoring_notes: This account scores in the 'Strong' range (7.2/10) for structural resemblance to genuine traumatic/anomalous memory. Key strengths: sensory specificity is high and multi-channel; spontaneity and authenticity markers are robust (self-corrections, genuine uncertainty, unprompted details, non-linear structure); internal consistency is solid with minor plausible memory artifacts; context

IC.rationale: Account is largely internally consistent with stable core details across multiple retellings. Minor uncertainty about tail ('I still to this day cannot recall if I saw a tail') is authentically acknowledged and fits genuine memory patterns. Timeline is coherent: September/August dusk, four teenagers walking, creature emerges, car horn, creature's gaze-and-decision moment, creature runs into woods, witnesses jog away, regroup at house. One potential minor inconsistency: Justin initially says 'around September into August' (unclear phrasing) but context suggests early evening in late summer. Slight ambiguity about whether creature saw them or car first, but Justin acknowledges this uncertainty naturally ('I don't know if it saw us first'). The 5-6 year avoidance of woods after is consistent with trauma response. No major contradictions detected.

I don't know if it saw us first and was stopping or if it saw the car first and was stopping
for about five years we we did not go back into the trails for about five for about five to six years

SS.rationale: Rich multi-sensory detail across 3-4 channels. Visual detail is extensive and specific: 'six and a half to seven feet tall,' 'erect ears,' 'pronounced snout,' 'muscle details in its thighs,' 'biceps,' 'forked legs and feet of a dog,' 'hands not paws,' 'darker skin' visible on torso, fur-covered head/neck/legs. Audio detail: 'howl slash guttural growl,' 'something I have never in my life ever heard,' car horn. Kinesthetic/proprioceptive: freeze response, hair standing up (mentioned later), jogging/sprinting away. Strong peripheral detail: they were 'walking around smoking cigarettes,' specific location (county side with no street lights, 'dusky' lighting, car with headlights coming). Environmental context well-established. Slight gap in olfactory channel (no smell reported), but overall sensory texture is substantial and specific. Lighting conditions explicitly detailed enabling clear observation.

you could see the muscle details in its thighs. I mean, you could see almost its biceps in a way

we were all standing all four in a row you know just chit-chatting and you know we noticed a car that was coming down the street

SA.rationale: Moderate spontaneity with some unprompted details and natural self-corrections. Justin volunteers contextual detail about the 1700s settlement, cemeteries, and witchcraft folklore without being asked—suggests authentic associative memory linking. He naturally admits uncertainty about the tail and about whether creature saw car or them first. He self-corrects on details ('I would almost want to say that it looked like it could have got down on all fours'). However, the account is also largely reactive to the host's structured questions, following a fairly linear narrative arc. Not heavily scripted, but interview format constrains spontaneity. Some narrative non-linearity when returning to the historical settlement tangent. Limited 'I know how this sounds' type defensive statements, though closing statement ('Please take me at my word') suggests awareness of credibility challenge. Self-correction about friends' long-term effects indicates reflexive honesty.
evidence_quotes: I still to this day cannot recall if I saw a tail or not
I don't know if it saw us first and was stopping or if it saw the car first and was stopping
I would almost want to say that it looked like it could have got down on all fours
I believe it was the Anderson family. And they had a lot of young kids die and everything like that. And there's, you know, cemeteries back there, old, old cemeteries from the 1700s
My friend Phillip, who actually his parents still live in this neighborhood, he never wanted to talk about it

EA.rationale: Emotional content is present and anchored to narratively appropriate moments, meeting 7-8 criteria. Fear/awe response clearly tied to specific trigger: the creature's gaze and intelligence display ('that's where I saw the intelligence. I saw it was trying to make a decision'). Physiologically authentic stress response: 'that sort of primordial fear kicked in,' 'hair on the back of your neck stands up' (mentioned in context of lasting aftermath). Emotional impact on peer group documented: one friend (Shelby) 'almost frozen in place, you know, we almost couldn't get her to even start walking again,' another friend (Phillip) suppressed and refused discussion. Meaningful disclosure delay (15 years from encounter to public account) suggests genuine processing burden. Long-term life change evident: 5-6 year avoidance of woods; ongoing hypervigilance when hunting ('when you hear that twig break... the hair on the back of your neck stands up'). However, emotion display is somewhat restrained in interview format (no voice breaks, crying, or dramatic vocal shifts apparent in transcript). Emotional authenticity is demonstrated through narrative coherence and post-impact description rather than affective performance.
evidence_quotes: that's where I saw the intelligence. I saw it was trying to make a decision of what I need to do next. That's what I perceived from it
that sort of primordial fear kicked in
the girl that was with the, she almost frozen place, you know, we almost couldn't get her to even start walking again after it had run off
the memory always kind of comes back. And, you know, the hair on the back of your neck stands up
for about five years we we did not go back into the trails for about five for about five to six years
when you hear that twig break or, you know, and you can't see anything around you, but you're in the middle of the woods. And that memory always kind of comes back

CP.rationale: Context is highly plausible and internally consistent. Legitimate reason to be there: four teenagers walking around their rural subdivision at dusk, a normal teenage behavior in rural Tennessee. Specific, verifiable geography: Jackson, West Tennessee, near Land Between the Lakes area, 1.5-2 hours away, specific road names (Cooper Anderson Road), documented historical settlement from 1700s. Spatial layout is coherent: county side with no street lights, fields, woodline behind houses, thin strip of woods between two roads, paintball course previously established, old cemeteries and ruins documented by local historians. Season/time coherence: September/August dusk (sun still partially up, car headlights on, dusky but visible). Activity/timing coherent: teenagers smoking cigarettes, normal neighborhood evening walk, car coming home. No implausible elements detected. Historical context (1700s settlement, witchcraft reports, cemeteries) adds legitimacy and is presented as locally verifiable. Host's corroboration that similar settlements exist validates this contextual layer.
evidence_quotes: I live in Jackson. So, you know, I would say it around two hours, you know, away. So not, not too far away, not too far away, you know, maybe about an hour and a half away from Kentucky Lake
it was just a normal you know day around in september into august and it was just you know going towards dusk
me and three other of my buddies we all just decided to walk around our neighborhood
we were all hanging out and we were around 15 16 years old and you know we were walking around smoking cigarettes

there's an old old, I guess, settlement from the middle to late 1700s. And there's, you know, a lot of, if anyone wants to look it up, it's Cooper and it's right off Cooper Anderson Road here in West Tennessee
it's still just the same, you know, small rural subdivision with, you know, the same exact fields around it

CO.rationale: Single but meaningful corroboration factor: three additional eyewitnesses present (Phillip, Brock, Shelby). Justin documents their corroboration: 'we all saw the same thing and we all did and we all just started talking about what was it what could it be.' Follow-up questioning confirms all four witnessed consistent details. However, Justin does not report that these witnesses came forward or gave independent testimony—only his own account is presented. Brock reportedly discussed it for years with Justin, suggesting consistency, but Phillip refused to discuss it. No documented report to authorities, no physical evidence presented, no prior area reports explicitly corroborated by external sources (though Justin mentions local rumors from childhood about LBL creatures). Host mentions speaking with other Tennessee witnesses but does not cross-reference this specific encounter. Historical settlement is verifiable by locals ('if anyone wants to look it up, it's Cooper') but not independently confirmed in this transcript. Corroboration is real but limited to peer witness testimony without independent documentation.
evidence_quotes: it was me and three other of my buddies we all just decided to walk around our neighborhood
we all four got back to my house and all just kind of sat down in disbelief and we were all just sort of asking if we all saw the same thing and we all did
me and my friend Brock went to the same high school and everything for the next three more years and everything. And me and him would always bring it up and talk about it
my friend Phillip, who actually his parents still live in this neighborhood, he never wanted to talk about it

notable_patterns: Delayed public disclosure (15 years post-encounter) consistent with genuine trauma processing and social disbelief concerns
Selective friend response: one peer avoidance (Phillip), one persistent discussion partner (Brock), one severely affected (Shelby)—authentic differential impact pattern
Creature intelligence perception as fear amplifier rather than reduction—authentic sophisticated threat assessment
Authentic admission of uncertainty (tail, creature's initial target) without defensive elaboration
Physiological stress markers present: primordial fear, prolonged hypervigilance, 5-6 year behavioral avoidance, hair-standing response
Contextual enrichment with verifiable local history (1700s settlement, witchcraft lore, cemeteries) presented as associative memory rather than narrative padding
Strong sensory specificity with lighting conditions explicitly controlled (dusky, headlights, still-visible detail)
Absence of elaborate post-encounter investigation narrative—simple regroup-and-discuss pattern typical of shocked witnesses
minimisation_phrases: you know, I'm not afraid to go out in the woods or, you know, I'm not afraid to go deer hunting
it's just, it's just something that always kind of sticks in the back of my mind
I just kind of had to keep it, you know, just kind of to myself and go about my, you know, day to day life
accounts.scoring_notes: Account scores in STRONG band (7.4/10 WCS). Emotional authenticity is weighted 25% per rubric; this account demonstrates genuine post-trauma markers (disclosure delay, peer differential response, hypervigilance, 5-6 year woods avoidance) anchored to

IC.rationale: This is not a witness testimony transcript at all. It is a podcast HOST INTRODUCTION and interview setup featuring two podcast producers/investigators (Brian Terrell and Coleman Ashley) discussing their research methods, documentary projects, and community efforts. The transcript contains no first-person experiential account of an anomalous encounter. The only embedded witness account is second-hand hearsay (the father's suspected dogman-induced heart attack, reported indirectly by the son to the host). No direct trauma narrative exists to evaluate for internal consistency.
evidence_quotes: Thanks for having me. Really appreciate you being here.
I live in Oklahoma. I grew up here, pretty much lived in Oklahoma my whole life. Had a few encounters when I was younger...
we were filming ironically for you know our bigfoot documentary and um you know that's the whole story in and of itself all the weird stuff that happened while filming that

SS.rationale: The transcript contains almost no sensory-rich direct experiential narrative. Brian's brief mention of his own past encounter ('more like a giant wolf') is vague and non-specific ('wasn't even sure what I was looking at'). The anecdote about the eyewitness in northeastern Oklahoma contains minimal sensory detail: 'dark silhouette but still clear outline,' 'long, exaggerated pointy ears,' and 'his voice started to shake.' The hearsay account of the father's death is purely speculative with no sensory grounding. No multi-channel sensory engagement (smell, touch, kinesthetic response) is described. Peripheral context entirely absent.
evidence_quotes: Mine wasn't even sure what I was looking at. Like a lot of people. I was pretty fortunate, though. Mine wasn you know looking like a Van Helsing werewolf It was just more like a giant wolf

he looked out his window and he saw the dark silhouette but still clear outline of essentially a dog man. And I believe it was moving in all fours at first. And then it got near his family's driveway. And he saw this thing stand up. And he talked about the long, exaggerated pointy ears
his voice started to shake

SA.rationale: The hosts and investigators are engaging in reactive discussion format, answering questions posed by Vic (the show host). There is minimal spontaneous disclosure of personal experience. Brian's past encounter is mentioned in response to introduction questions, not volunteered unprompted. Self-corrections exist ('I want to say 72. I could be wrong on that') but these are colloquial hedging, not genuine memory uncertainty confessions. No 'I know how this sounds' type authenticity markers appear. The narrative is structured, linear, and professional—typical of podcast guests discussing their research, not trauma witnesses reliving difficult memories. Coleman's reflection on hypothetical preparedness for encounter ('Yeah, I've definitely thought about that') is thoughtful but prospective, not retrospective.
evidence_quotes: I want to say 72. I could be wrong on that. Okay.
you know, I'm going based off memory here. So some details, you know, maybe a little vague.
And for years I didn know what I actually seen And but you know eventually I ended up because I was interested in Sasquatch I ended up bumbling into the whole dogman phenomenon

EA.rationale: No genuine emotional authenticity markers present in any direct first-person account. The closest emotional moment is Vic's account of the father's death anecdote—emotionally significant but delivered in measured, cautious tone ('I'm not going to go into any more details... I'm going to kind of walk on eggshells here'). Brian and Coleman discuss emotional impacts abstractly ('I've had conversations with Brian even a few times where we've talked about whether it be Dogman or Sasquatch or any cryptid... you hear all the stories of people having real PTSD') but show no emotional breaks in their own delivery. Coleman's reflection on hypothetical fear is intellectual, not visceral. No voice quality shifts, no pause-and-recover moments, no disclosure delays. Professional, controlled affect throughout. The sole embedded second-hand account (the Northeastern Oklahoma eyewitness) shows emotional resonance ('his voice started to shake') but this is reported by Coleman, not directly evidenced.
evidence_quotes: I'm not going to go into any more details and just say that he got the feeling that something wasn't right
And he talked about the long, exaggerated pointy ears that, you know, you hear about a lot when it comes to these encounters. And his voice started to shake.
when it comes to like a class a full clear as day close encounter I haven had a chance to have that really yet

CP.rationale: Context is plausible for the meta-narrative (podcast interviews, field investigations in Oklahoma, documentary production) but geographically and situationally loose. Brian's background (Oklahoma lifelong resident, encounters in youth, predator hunting experience) is coherent. Coleman's involvement (filmmaker-to-researcher pipeline starting 3 years ago with documentary, joining Red Dirt in November 2022) is internally consistent. The secondary accounts (Northeastern Oklahoma eyewitness, Lawton werewolf incident, father's death) lack geographic specificity ('can't reveal exact location'). The anecdote about the father has logical inconsistencies: the son's sense that 'something wasn't right' prompts a house visit; father found dead; autopsy confirms heart attack; but no clear causative evidence for dogman involvement is provided ('we don't know for sure'). The Lawton werewolf anecdote (72/74, man saw one through window, had heart attack, survived, told story) is historically vague and cannot be verified from transcript.
evidence_quotes: And obviously we can't reveal, you know, individual's name or exact location or whatever. It was in Northeastern Oklahoma.
There were certain things that this dogman eyewitness found, however, that point towards it being a dogman that his father had told him about coming around his house way out in the country.
we don't know for sure that that's what caused his heart attack

CO.rationale: No external corroboration present for any direct experiential account attributed to Brian or Coleman. Brian's past encounters are uncorroborated self-reports. The Northeastern Oklahoma eyewitness account is reported second-hand by Coleman citing a video by Brian on Red Dirt's YouTube channel—but the actual witness testimony is not in this transcript and the primary source is unavailable for evaluation. The father's death is a reported third-hand anecdote (son told Vic, Vic reports to audience). No other witnesses present. No medical records, autopsy details, or independent verification provided. The historical Lawton werewolf case is cited vaguely without source attribution. No physical evidence (prints, audio spectrograph analysis) is conclusively presented—Brian mentions audio 'on spectrograph' but explicitly withholds analysis ('I'm not going to really elaborate on that just yet'). The nine-inch track claim is anecdotal (someone reached out, camera died, photo lost). Zero corroboration achieved.
evidence_quotes: there's a video of it on the YouTube channel too as well, where we interviewed this person. He was off camera

i had a guy reach out to me from south of tecumseh who claimed that his neighbor had taken a picture of a nine inch canine track... the camera that this photo was on uh it died or it quit and they just it was a backed up to me they lost it forever
we don't know for sure that that's what caused his heart attack

notable_patterns: Transcript is not a witness testimony. It is a podcast host interview with two investigators/researchers discussing their fieldwork methods and projects.
No direct first-person trauma account is present for scoring.
All embedded accounts are second- or third-hand anecdotes reported by investigators, not primary witness testimony.
Professional, measured affect throughout—appropriate for podcast guests, inappropriate for trauma disclosure.

Explicit uncertainty and hedging ('I'm going based off memory here... maybe a little vague'; 'I want to say 72. I could be wrong'; 'we don't know for sure') appears colloquial rather than authentic memory struggle.

Investigators demonstrate responsible epistemic caution ('I don't want to be quoted 10 years from now saying something stupid'), which is intellectually sound but undermines emotional authenticity of embedded anecdotes.
Historical cases cited without source documentation or verification pathway.
Anecdotal evidence explicitly lost or inaccessible (photo from died camera, exact location withheld).
Structural pattern: investigators frame themselves as cautious, truth-seeking, community-building actors—a professional identity construction rather than trauma survivor testimony.
minimisation_phrases: I'm going based off memory here
So some details, you know, maybe a little vague
I want to say 72. I could be wrong on that
I don't have those yet, but I'm working on it
accounts.scoring_notes: CRITICAL CONTEXT: This transcript does not contain a witness testimony for evaluation. It is a podcast episode featuring the host (Vic Cundiff of Dogman Encounters Radio) interviewing two cryptid researchers (Brian Terrell and Coleman Ashley). The scoring exercise presumes a witness trauma narrative; this document delivers a professional discussion of research methodology, community building, and secondhand anecdotes. The only embedded first-person accounts are: (1) Brian's vague childhood encounter ('just more like a giant wolf'), and (2) Coleman's prospective reflection on hypothetical encounter preparedness. Neither constitutes a coherent trauma narrative. All emotionally significant cases (Northeastern Oklahoma eyewitness, father's suspected dogman-induced death, Lawton werewolf incident) are reported as hearsay, with documentation either unavailable (off-camera interview), withheld (location anonymization), or lost (photograph from dead camera). The affect throughout is professional, intellectually cautious, and community-focused—structurally incompatible with authentic trauma disclosure. The research framing (seeking solid answers, avoiding premature conclusions, building bridges in the community) reflects responsible paranormal investigation practice but fundamentally opposes the spontaneity and emotional authenticity expected of genuine witness testimony. Scoring reflects this categorical mismatch: the transcript cannot be evaluated as trauma memory because it contains no unmediated trauma narrative.
